title: Fitting a bath panel
subtitle: Cut the frame, fix the panel, fit the clips
excerpt: How to fit a bath side panel: measuring the opening, building a simple batten frame to carry the panel, cutting the panel to fit, and fixing it with removable clips so the plumbing beneath remains accessible.
sourceNotes: Standard UK bath panel installation practice. Collins Complete DIY Manual. Bath panel and batten framing technique from plumbing trade practice.

content.text: A bath panel closes the gap between the rim of the bath and the floor and wall. Most ready-made panels are sold as the board plus a clip system; the job is to build a batten frame to support the board and to cut the board accurately to fit the opening. The panel must be removable: the plumbing connections under the bath need to be reached when a tap or waste fitting needs work.
Measuring
Step 1, measure the opening.

Measure the height from the underside of the bath rim to the floor at three points along the run. Measure the width. On an acrylic bath the rim may not be level; the panel must follow the lowest point of the rim. Check the bath rim level with the spirit level.
Building the batten frame
Step 2, cut and fix the battens.

Cut two horizontal 50 × 25 mm battens: one at floor level, one about 50 mm below the bath rim. Fix the upper batten to the wall using screws into plugged holes, or to the bath frame if the manufacturer provides fixing brackets. Fix the lower batten to the floor with screws or, on a tiled floor, with construction adhesive. The panel will clip onto or screw to these battens.
Cutting and fitting the panel
Step 3, cut the panel to size.

Mark the panel at the measured height and width. Cut with a fine-tooth jigsaw blade (face-down if the panel is melamine-faced, to avoid chipping the visible surface). A circular saw against a straightedge gives a cleaner rip cut if available. Cut-off panels can be removed from the upper edge; make sure the cut goes inside the bath rim overhang so the cut edge is hidden.
Step 4, fit the clips or screws.

Most bath panel clip systems use a clip fixed to the batten and a matching socket pressed into the back face of the panel. Align the sockets with the clips and press the panel home. Test that the panel can be released for access. If the panel is a simple screw-fix type, drive screws from the panel face into the battens and cover the screw heads with matching colour caps.
Sealing the edges
Step 5, seal the junction with the bath rim.

The gap between the top of the panel and the underside of the bath rim needs sealing with silicone sealant to prevent water getting behind the panel. Apply a bead of sanitary silicone (white or colour-matched) along the gap, smooth with a wet finger, and allow to cure before wetting.
Common mistakes
Panel rattles or bows.
The battens are not close enough to the panel face, or the panel is cut slightly too wide and is under compression. Ease the cut edge with a plane or sanding block and re-fit.
Gap between the panel and the floor.
The floor is uneven. Scribe the bottom of the panel to follow the floor using a compass set to the widest gap, run along the floor while marking the panel, then trim along the mark.
